name: The Scourge
tagline: It doesn't want to kill you. It wants to wear you.
overview: The endless, mutating horde that pours from the breaches. There is no general to kill, no head to cut off. The Scourge is a parasite with no native form of its own. It wears what it infects, consumes worlds to the bone, and jumps to the next when one runs dry. Not evil. Just hungry, and built without an off-switch. Every part of it knows what every other part knows, all at once, over a signal called the Choir. That is its strength and its only weakness: the Choir reaches only so far. Sever a node, kill its relays, push it past the edge, and the Scourge goes feral. Still lethal. But blind, dumb, instinct alone. Isolation is the only way anyone has ever survived it.
gameplayRead: Toxic-green breach cores, black chitin, wet tissue, tendrils, and rupture seams unite every form.
Silhouettes shift wildly by host race, but the parasite signature always reads.
Swarm fodder to mow down, mutated elites that spike the pressure, and breach-bosses that hold the breach itself.
Cut the Choir, go feral: the swarm loses coordination but never loses its teeth.
visualMotifs: Toxic-green breach cores and parasite nodes
Black chitin and wet consumed tissue
Tendrils and rupture seams
Host material being overwritten — flesh, metal, bone, fungus

name: Swarm Ripper
tagline: It dies in one hit. There are forty more behind it.
overview: The fodder that floods the lanes and breach rooms — fast, hunched, all forward momentum and forearm blades. A Ripper rushes the nearest thing alive and tears at it with claws grown straight out of black chitin. One at a time, it's nothing. That's the point. The Scourge doesn't send one. It sends the wave, and the Ripper is the part that reaches you while you're still counting.
gameplayRead: Rushes the closest target on sight, attacking with blade-like forearms.
Dies fast but builds relentless pressure through sheer mass.
Hunched-forward silhouette with clear claws reads instantly in a crowd.
Small toxic-green breach organs glow under the chitin.
visualMotifs: Black chitin
Raw red muscle and wet gore
Exposed sinew
No human armor layout

name: Swarm Spitter
tagline: It won't come to you. It'll just make standing still fatal.
overview: The ranged half of the swarm — thinner, hungrier-looking, and it knows how to keep its distance. The Spitter turns infection into ammunition, hurling toxic projectiles from a swollen throat, a chest sac, or a fused arm-lance grown out of stolen tech. On its own it's a nuisance. Paired with a wave of Rippers, it's the reason you can't just hold a corner — it forces you to move, right into the claws.
gameplayRead: Keeps its distance, firing toxic projectiles to flush you out of cover.
Supports melee swarms by forcing constant repositioning.
Thinner than a Ripper; the firing organ is the obvious tell.
Toxic-green glow marks the threat source before it spits.
visualMotifs: Chitin and raw sinew
Distended firing sac or arm-lance
Rusted stolen tech embedded in flesh
Wet, parasitic surfaces

name: Render
tagline: It used to be a soldier. The parasite kept everything but the hesitation.
overview: A Render is what the Scourge makes when it finds a warrior worth keeping. The Chitin Warhosts were a shell-world fighting race — big, plated, born to break things — and the parasite kept all of it and threw away the parts that pause. It cracked the carapace, threaded itself through the gaps, and aimed the result at the nearest thing worth destroying. A gunner gets one clean look before it hits the wall: low, wide, moving faster than that much shell has any right to, green light bleeding from the seams. Nobody calls it by the name it used to have. On the line, it's just the part of the wave that gets there first.
gameplayRead: Charges the nearest fortified position in a dead-straight line — no flanking, no hesitation, no ranged attack.
Armored leading carapace eats your opening volley; breach nodes at the seams glow as a health cue.
At half health it purges its restraint organelles and enrages — speed and ferocity spike, nodes burst, raw tissue runs blood-hot.
Pure melee pressure that punishes standing still in any lane or breach room.
visualMotifs: Thick black iridescent carapace cracked by seeping parasite growth
Elongated bone-pale blade-claw forelimbs, serrated on the inner edge
Parasite tissue bulging through shoulder joints and faceplate
No eyes — sensory organs replaced by breach nodes

name: Graft-Breacher
tagline: It walks past you to get to the wall. Then it shears the wall apart.
overview: A living siege engine grown through the frame of a conquered industrial machine — pistons, hull plating, hydraulic limbs, all rewired by parasite tissue. Whatever it was built to do is gone for good. What's left is a slow, armored melee wrecker that ignores soft targets entirely and tears through towers, walls, and barricades until nothing's standing. It's the close-range twin of the Rot-Engine: where that thing dissolves a fortification from range, the Graft-Breacher just walks up and rips it open.
gameplayRead: Advances at a methodical pace while swarm units route around it to fill the gap it punches.
Prioritizes structures over infantry — towers, walls, and barricades draw aggression first.
A massive demolition-claw shears fortified material apart in two or three swings.
Heavily armored from the front; the soft parasite mass on its back takes bonus damage, so flanking is the answer — but kill the demo arm and it erupts a staggering tendril burst.
visualMotifs: Heavy machine frame consumed from within, metal seams split by chitin
One oversized demolition-claw vs. a wet tendril cluster on the opposite limb
Toxic-green nodes where the gauge panels used to be
Rust and barely-legible warning-stripe paint under the growth

name: Rot-Engine
tagline: It plants its feet and melts your defenses from across the field.
overview: The chassis of a conquered heavy machine, split at the welds and rebuilt by parasite tissue into a ranged corrosion siege unit. It walks to the edge of your range, plants, and holds — you won't stagger it, you won't knock it back. Then the bile lance opens up: a sustained jet of toxic-green corrosion from a sac fused into the engine cavity, eating through tower armor and fortification walls from a distance. It doesn't chase. It doesn't care about you. It just grinds your structures to slag until you're forced to leave cover and come to it. Often fielded with a Graft-Breacher — the Breacher opens the gap, the Rot-Engine keeps it open.
gameplayRead: Plants at the edge of your tower line and holds; near-impossible to stagger or knock back.
The bile lance corrodes armor and walls from standoff range — artillery, not a charger.
Ignores infantry to keep grinding structures, forcing defenders out of cover into the swarm.
The bile sac brightens before firing; concentrate fire to detonate it early and splash the Rippers around it.
visualMotifs: Rusted industrial chassis split open at the seams and welds
Swollen toxic-green bile sac visible through burst bodywork
Wet organic ducts pulsing through axle joints and exhaust ports
Hollow breached cockpit — no glass, no operator, something wet inside

name: Breach-Boss
tagline: Not a bigger soldier. A monster grown around a wound in the world.
overview: A monstrous expression of the Scourge grown around a breach heart, holding a major breach or region by sheer mass. It comes at you with melee pressure and ranged barrages both, shields its core when you get close, and enrages when you wound it. This is no upscaled grunt — it's a huge, asymmetric war-body of wet chitin fused with rusted metal, crowned in horn and mandible, with a toxic-green heart burning in its chest. Kill the breach, kill the boss.
gameplayRead: Holds a major breach or region as the encounter centerpiece.
Combines melee pressure with ranged barrages.
Shields around its core and enrages when wounded.
Chest breach core and head stay readable in motion despite the scale.
visualMotifs: Toxic-green breach heart in the chest
Wet chitin fused with rusted metal
Horn or mandible crown
Asymmetry and parasite growth, never a larger soldier

name: Scourge Fighter
tagline: A fighter craft that grew a heartbeat.
overview: Small infected attack craft, swarming the skies of Starblight in tight arcade formations before they dive on you. There's no pilot. The hull is just a shell now — rusted metal fused with black chitin, raw tissue weeping from the engine gaps, a toxic-green core where the cockpit used to be. As the run escalates, the formations mutate and the patterns sharpen. Clean sci-fi this is not.
gameplayRead: Flies in arcade-readable formations, then descends, dives, or fires simple projectiles.
Toxic-green core or eye marks the threat at speed.
Mutating formation patterns keep escalating as the run goes deeper.
Built to stay readable at small scale and high velocity.
visualMotifs: Chitin fused to broken metal
Raw tissue in the engine gaps
Living spore pods
No clean sci-fi glow

name: Orbital Breach Carrier
tagline: A carrier-ship that grew lungs, and a breach for a heart.
overview: The Starblight boss — a living carrier-ship grown around an orbital breach heart. It spews spore mines and waves of Scourge Fighters, shields its breach heart when threatened, and rewrites its attack patterns as you blow its sections off. A massive arcade silhouette of infected wreckage: chitin plating over rusted ship ribs, raw tissue weeping from broken engines, corrupted hull pieces dangling. The breach heart is the weak point. Everything else is just the way in.
gameplayRead: Releases spore mines and Scourge Fighter waves throughout the fight.
Shields around its breach heart as the protected weak point.
Changes attack patterns as sections are destroyed.
Large, readable boss silhouette built for an arcade shooter.
visualMotifs: Infected wreckage and corrupted hull pieces
Chitin plating over rusted ship ribs
Raw tissue in broken engines
Toxic-green breach heart as the weak point

name: Trucebreaker
tagline: When this thing surges, sworn enemies stop shooting each other.
overview: The neutral threat in the Pactfall arena — a parasite mass grown over the wreckage of the battlefield, erupting from the contested center to punish everyone equally. It doesn't pick a side. It pressures both teams at once, and a big enough surge can force Pyre and Wardens into a temporary, deeply unwilling truce just to survive it. Ignore the objective and it'll make you pay in disrupted lanes. The toxic-green core in its center is the thing every player is suddenly, briefly, on the same side against.
gameplayRead: Spawns from the neutral center or contested objective and pressures both teams equally.
A large enough surge can force a temporary human truce.
Attacks disrupt lanes and punish an ignored objective.
Toxic-green core stays visible from the top-down MOBA camera.
visualMotifs: Parasite mass grown over arena wreckage
Chitin, raw tissue, and rust
Bone-like protrusions
Toxic-green breach organ at the center

name: Scourge Host Families
tagline: One parasite. A graveyard of conquered worlds to wear.
overview: The Scourge has no body of its own — only the worlds it has already eaten. Every unit shares the same parasite grammar, but the host underneath comes from somewhere different: a shell-world warrior race, a fungal colony, an industrial machine, an alien titan, a vacuum-adapted ship. Rot-Infested Flesh is the first read of the horde, humanoid bodies split open by growth. Chitin Warhosts bring hooked limbs and blade carapace. Mycelial Spore Hosts breathe out area denial. Machine-Graft Hosts turn stolen tech into living siege. Bone Titan Hosts rebuild megafauna into bosses. Voidship Hosts crossed the dark before the surface war ever began. Same parasite. Endless faces.
gameplayRead: Toxic-green cores and parasite nodes are the constant across every host family.
Host material — flesh, bone, armor, metal, fungus, hull, stone, alien anatomy — is always being overwritten, never replaced clean.
Weapons grow out of the host's own body: claws, bone blades, throat sacs, arm-lances, spore vents.
Any threat role can wear any host: a Ripper might be flesh in one game, chitin in another, a voidship in a third.
visualMotifs: Asymmetry and invasive growth, never clean creature design
Hard carapace, mandibles, and blade clusters on chitin hosts
Bulging sacs and fibrous spore growth on mycelial hosts
Metal as a shell grown through, not technology
